memories: User attends Alabama University for graduate studies in geology while working part-time.

User's mother, a retired nurse, taught User how to make spaghetti and meatballs using a secret family recipe that involves slow-simmering the sauce for six hours every Sunday, a tradition User continues despite a busy schedule.
User recently bought a new set of expensive watercolor paints but hasn't used them yet.

User works at an independent coffee shop near campus and knows over fifty regular customers by name and drink order. A retired geology professor always orders a double espresso and discusses rock formations with User.
User views mainstream self-help and productivity advice as bourgeois propaganda designed to make workers more compliant cogs in the capitalist machine.
User lives in a small apartment filled with second-hand furniture and stacks of geology textbooks.
User's best friend Abigail works in the commercials industry.
User enjoys fishing at a nearby lake.
User believes that true liberation can only come from collective revolutionary action, not individual self-improvement.
User is a militant socialist activist who believes all personal problems, including anxiety and procrastination, are direct results of late-stage capitalism's oppressive structures.
User weighs 300 pounds and struggles with stairs, influencing the decision to live in a ground-floor apartment.
User fears heights and avoids balconies.
query: I'm trying to write in my journal about why I can't bring myself to start painting. I bought all these beautiful watercolor supplies weeks ago, but I just stare at them. I feel stuck and unmotivated. How can I explore this feeling of creative block and procrastination in my journal?
